coredata.dc:title: Machine learning for design of thin-film nanocomposite membranes
coredata.dc:creator: Fetanat, Masoud
Keshtiara, Mohammadali
Keyikoglu, Ramazan
Khataee, Alireza
Daiyan, Rahman
Razmjou, Amir
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel machine learning approach is proposed for estimation of the permeate flux and foulant rejection in nanocomposite filtration membranes. Nine independent variables are fed to artificial neural networks (ANNs) including support, nanoparticles concentration, concentration of organic phase trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in-n-hexane (TMC in n-hexane), operation pressure, contact angle, thin layer thickness, location of the nanoparticles (NPs), post-treatment temperature and duration, and permeate flux and foulant rejection were derived as the outputs of the ANNs. The proposed method was evaluated on two datasets across training, validation and test datasets, and an unseen dataset. 2250 different initial weights and number of the neurons in the hidden layer for the proposed ANN models were considered and compared to find the optimized ANN models. The mean squared error (MSE) and coefficient of determination (R2) were employed to select the best 20 ANN models for further analysis. The proposed ANN models resulted in accurate estimates for both permeate flux and foulant rejection with R2 of 0.9958 and 0.9412 in all data included in the training, validation and test datasets and R2 of 0.9938 and 0.9811 in unseen dataset, respectively. In addition, results of sensitivity analysis revealed that post treatment temperature and contact angle were found the most important input variables for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection. The proposed method can provide valuable insights for formulating permeate flux and foulant rejection and considering the effects of each experimental condition on nanocomposite filtration membranes without doing real experiments, which is time-consuming and expensive.

Sensitivity analysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel machine learning approach is proposed for estimation of the permeate flux and foulant rejection in nanocomposite filtration membranes. Nine independent variables are fed to artificial neural networks (ANNs) including support, nanoparticles concentration, concentration of organic phase trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in-n-hexane (TMC in n-hexane), operation pressure, contact angle, thin layer thickness, location of the nanoparticles (NPs), post-treatment temperature and duration, and permeate flux and foulant rejection were derived as the outputs of the ANNs. The proposed method was evaluated on two datasets across training, validation and test datasets, and an unseen dataset. 2250 different initial weights and number of the neurons in the hidden layer for the proposed ANN models were considered and compared to find the optimized ANN models. The mean squared error (MSE) and coefficient of determination (R2) were employed to select the best 20 ANN models for further analysis. The proposed ANN models resulted in accurate estimates for both permeate flux and foulant rejection with R2 of 0.9958 and 0.9412 in all data included in the training, validation and test datasets and R2 of 0.9938 and 0.9811 in unseen dataset, respectively. In addition, results of sensitivity analysis revealed that post treatment temperature and contact angle were found the most important input variables for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection. The proposed method can provide valuable insights for formulating permeate flux and foulant rejection and considering the effects of each experimental condition on nanocomposite filtration membranes without doing real experiments, which is time-consuming and expensive.

ce:section.ce:para: Thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes are widely used in reverse osmosis (RO) and nanofiltration (NF) processes since they offer high flux and rejection along with mechanical stability. TFN membranes are mostly produced by forming a polyamide layer (PA) on a porous polymer substrate through interfacial polymerization (IP) process, during which polycondensation occurs between one monomer in aqueous solution and another monomer in an organic solution [1]. Nevertheless, the performance of TFN RO or NF membranes are limited to the trade-off relationship between the water permeability and membrane selectivity [1]. Thus, many attempts have been made to improve the performance of TFN membranes such as making modifications to the membrane support or the PA layer [1]. Moreover, surface modifications using hydrophilic additives [2] and co-solvent addition [3] have also been carried out [4]. Among these approaches, incorporation of nanoparticles (NPs) as nanofillers in the selective PA to produce so-called thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes have seen increased research interests in the last decade [5]. Increased water permeability has been reported due to the formation of additional channels through the microporosity of nanoparticles [6] while also maintaining or even increasing the rejection performance.

Since the first use of zeolite 4A NPs in 2007 by Jeong et al. [5], various kinds of NPs of different composition and shapes have been embedded in the polyamide layer of thin-film membranes including carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [7], graphene oxide (GO) [8], silica [9], Titanium dioxide (TiO2) [10], layer double hydroxides (LDHs) [11], graphitic carbon nitride (gCN) [12], metal organic frameworks [13]), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [14]. The introduction of such nanoparticles into the PA layer modifies properties such as the hydrophilicity, porosity, thickness, density, and surface zeta potential [15]. Therefore, with suitable nanoparticles, membranes can acquire beneficial properties such as adsorption, antibacterial characteristic, photocatalytic activity, or antifouling [15]. The physical and chemical properties of nanoparticles have an important effect on the overall performance of TFN membranes. The solution into which NPs are added (organic or water), the loading rates, dimensions, solubilities can affect the interactions and their distribution in the polyamide layer.

For example, the key significance of NP content in the PA layer has been shown in literature, where the permeability of TFN membranes is reported to increase up to a specific NP concentration, and exceeding this threshold adversely affected both the permeability and selectivity [16]. It has been reported that the NPs with smaller size increases the separation performance while the larger NPs could cause faster membrane fouling due to the increased surface roughness [17]. On the other hand, NPs with even smaller sizes become more inclined to agglomeration [18]. Moreover, the thickness of the ultrathin polyamide layer (less than ~250 nm) plays a limiting factor in the incorporation of candidate NPs. Apart from the NPs, the conditions of the interfacial polymerization (IP) process such as the type of the monomers, monomer concentrations, organic solvent, IP reaction time, curing time, curing temperature, and the other additives can be varied to modify the PA layer [19]. The surface properties and pore structure of the support layer, on which the PA layer is formed, also affects the membrane performance [20].

Despite advances and insights from laboratory experiments and theoretical calculations and simulations, the scale-up of TFN membranes is restricted owing to several challenges. One concern is that the agglomeration of NPs in the PA layer leading to poor permeation and rejection efficiencies. Another challenge is the waste of rare or expensive NPs in the process of design and optimization, which may increase significantly the cost of scale-up process [6]. Finally, the interaction between the NPs and the solvent they are added should be taken into account since it affects the stable dispersion of NPs and the chemical bonding between NPs and PA layer [6]. In order for TFN membranes to find widespread applications in industrial scales, it is crucial to determine the suitable production parameters at which the water permeability and salt rejection are maximized. Currently, the development of TFN membranes involves numerous time-consuming and costly laboratory experiments concerning the separation performance tests, surface chemistry analysis, and membrane stability. Estimation of the outcomes of the membrane processes is necessary for the analysis and optimization of the process and for the design of new membrane processes. In this context, theoretical modeling techniques enable us to simulate membrane processes to help design such processes in a more efficient and economical way.

Given the high number of parameters and the complexity of the membrane processes, machine learning (ML) approaches can be valuable tools, which are increasingly applied in many systems having complex nature. The availability of many open-source libraries and algorithms along with the improvements in the field of artificial intelligence (AI) made the ML techniques more efficient [21]. Compared with the conventional modeling methods, which are limited by strict assumptions of normality, homogeneity, and variable independence, artificial neural networks (ANNs) are capable of predicting systems working in complex and uncertain systems [22]. ANNs have been utilized in various membrane preparation [23] and separation processes including NF [24], UF [25,26], RO [27], MF [28]. For instance, ANN has been employed to predict the rejection of NaCl and MgCl2 in the sweater using the NF membrane process [29]. In another study, Salehi and Razavi [30] reported that ANN with one hidden layer consisting of nine neurons best fits the experimental data, which enabled the prediction flux with a correlation coefficient of 0.96.

ML as a part of AI can learn a pattern from the previous data automatically and can respond appropriately to new data [31]. ML methods can be utilized to determine the complex relationships between the input and output data using nonlinear functions. Nevertheless, the performance of the estimation and generalization ability of the ML method depends on the chosen training method and architecture. Accordingly, the appropriate training algorithm and architecture of ML should be selected carefully [32]. ANNs basically link input and output data using a set of weights between the neurons, biases, linear, and nonlinear functions through a training phase. Training of ANN model is actually finding the optimized weights using an optimization algorithm to minimize a performance metric. A multi-layer feedforward neural network with one hidden layer can approximate any continuous nonlinear mapping function known as a universal approximation theorem. However, the number of neurons in the hidden layer, initial weights and biases, optimization method, and other settings should be carefully selected as they have a substantial influence on the performance of the ANN models [33].

Bayesian Regularization (BR) training algorithm minimizes a linear combination of squared errors between the target and output the ANN models. Yan et al. showed that training ANN models with BR method resulted in better estimation performance compared to Levenberg–Marquardt (LM) backpropagation training algorithm. In addition, the ANN models trained with BR training algorithm demonstrated superior performance on test and unseen datasets [34]. Moreover, an ANN model with one hidden layer and BR training algorithm outperforms other ML algorithms such as K-Star classifier [35], linear regression [36], support vector machine (SVM) with normalized polynomial kernel [37], SVM with radial basis function (RBF) kernel [37], nearest neighbor balltree [38], Gaussian process with polynomial kernel and Gaussian process with RBF kernel [39] for regression problems [40].

In this study, we proposed new multi-layer feedforward ANN models with one hidden layer and BR training algorithm for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection, in which initial weights and number of neurons in the hidden layer of the ANN models were optimized to minimize the error between the actual and output values of the models.

This work aims to design a method to formulate permeate flux and foulant rejection in nanocomposite filtration membranes based on the experimental variables, and consider the effects of each experimental condition on nanocomposite filtration membranes. The objective of this study is to estimate permeate flux and foulant rejection considering data from real experiments reported in literature to minimize time and expenses related to the experiments.

In the following sections, first the collected data and ANNs are introduced. Then, performance metrics, data normalization, optimization of ANN models and sensitivity analysis are described. Afterward, the result of statistical analysis and correlation between the ANN model inputs are presented. Subsequently, the results of the optimized ANN models on training, validation, test and unseen datasets are shown and compared with the experimental dataset. Finally, the order of importance of each input of the optimized ANN models are presented.

The aim of section is to collate and highlight the trends in research progress related to thin-film nanocomposites membranes during the past decade, from 2011 onwards. The main highlight is to emphasis on materials, structures, fabrication methods to incorporate different types of nanomaterials into TFN membranes for achieving better results than that of conventionally prepared TFN membrane. To this end, the academic databases (i.e., Google Scholar, Scopus, and Web of Science) have been used to collate academic articles using keywords such as TFN, Thin-film Nanocomposites. At the time of writing, there were 138 published journal articles related to the topic of TFN membranes [1,7–15,17,18,20,41–156] The TABLE 1 shows the statistical data (764 samples) of the input and output variables used as training, validation, test and unseen datasets in this study. Full data set can be shared upon request via a material transfer agreement.

ANNs are biological inspired computational models used for prediction, modeling, control, system identification, and classification [33]. ANN has been extensively used to solve complex problems in fields such as economics, engineering and medicine [33,157,158]. ANNs only require the input and output data to learn a pattern or model through the training phase. In the training phase, the weights and biases (the parameters of the ANN model) of each neuron (comprises two operators 1. summing junction and 2. activation function) of the ANN models are updated to minimize the error between the outputs (the estimated responses predicted by ANN-model) and the targets (the actual responses determined experimentally by observation). The trained ANN models are then applied to a new test data [33].

Fig. 1 shows the proposed ANN architecture including the input and output variables, input, hidden and output layers of the ANN models. The input variables are fed to the input layer of the ANN models and they are then followed by hidden layer where all the computation is performed. Finally, the output variables are derived from the output layer of the ANN models. In this study, nine input variables were selected to estimate the permeate flux and foulant rejection for nanocomposite filtration membranes as the outputs of the ANN model. The inputs of the ANN model were: 1. support, 2. particles concentration, 3. concentration of organic phase trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in-n-hexane (TMC in n-hexane), 4. operation pressure, 5. contact angle, 6. thin layer thickness, 7. location of the NPs within the filtration membrane, 8. post treatment temperature and 9. post treatment duration. From literature, support types were found in one of the 8 categories; “PSf”, “PES”, “PI”, “Si3N4”, “PSU”, “PEI”, “PAN” and ”PVDF”, which were encoded to numerical values by assigning a value of 1 to 8 to each of them respectively. Furthermore, location of the NPs within the filtration membrane were found from literature in one of the 7 categories; ‘Grafted on the TFC membrane’, ‘Organic solution’, ‘PVA solution’, ‘Aqueous solution’, ‘Membrane support’, ‘PSF support’, ‘Polymer support casting solution’, which were converted to numerical values by assigning a value of 0 to 6 to each of them respectively.

Optimization of ANN models using BR resulted in better performance compared with the LM training algorithm [159]. Therefore, in this study, BR backpropagation training algorithm was used to train the ANN models. Moreover, BR training algorithm is difficult to over-train or overfitted due to automatic procedures, which can stop updating the weights and biases [160]. In the training phase, the ANN model weights and biases were first selected randomly and then BR training algorithm adjusted the ANN weights and biases to minimize the error between the targets and outputs of the ANN model. The stopping criteria for training phase were the maximum number of iterations (1000) or automatic stopping criteria derived from BR training algorithm to prevent over-fitting problems. Hyperbolic tangent sigmoid activation function in the hidden layer and a linear function in the output layer was employed. The kth output neuron in the output layer (

The number of neurons in the hidden layers of the ANN models has a remarkable influence on the performance and therefore, it should be optimized. While more neurons in the hidden layer can improve training performance, it can make ANN models non-generalizable by increasing the probability of over-fitting and the likelihood of ANN models making errors in validation and test datasets. In addition, there is no systemic method for choosing the number of neurons in the hidden layer of the ANN models which can work for all the datasets [161]. Accordingly, different number of neurons in the hidden layer was evaluated to improve the training, validation and test performance metrics (MSE and R2).

Fig. 2 depicts the flowchart of the proposed ANN models for estimation of the permeate flux and foulant rejection. First, the number of neurons in the hidden layer was chosen between 5 and 50, then 80%, 10%, and 10% of the dataset were randomly selected as the training validation and test datasets respectively. Subsequently, MSE and R2 were calculated. The process was repeated to train the ANN models 50 times with different initial weights. The initial random weight was the only difference among the 50 independent runs with a specific number of neurons in hidden layer. Accordingly, the optimized number of the neurons in the hidden layer was determined using MSE and R2 on training, validation and test datasets. The lower MSE and higher R2 were employed to find the best ANN models.

In all the runs, BR training algorithm was chosen as the training method. Activation functions were set to hyperbolic tangent sigmoid for hidden layer and linear for output layer and the normalization method was set to min-max method.

Identifying the most significant input variables of the ANN models, which have the biggest effect on permeate flux and foulant rejection as the output of the ANN models, can be performed by sensitivity analysis. Accordingly, sensitivity analysis was used to determine the order of importance of input variables of the ANN models as follows: [161,162]

To have more reliable and stable results, a group of the best 20 ANN models created in the previous section was used to calculate the index of relative importance. For both permeate flux and foulant rejection, sensitivity analysis was applied to the best 20 ANN models and the average of the index of relative importance of each input variable of the ANN models was evaluated [161,163].

The Neural Network Toolbox was employed to optimize, evaluate and validate the ANN models on MATLAB (R2019b) platform. The ANN models were created from 258 and 225 samples of permeate flux and foulant rejection on training, validation and testing datasets respectively. In addition, 168 new samples for permeate flux and 113 new samples for foulant rejection were used as an unseen dataset.

ce:section.ce:section-title: An overview of a decade of thin-film nanocomposite membrane development

ce:section.ce:para: Thin-film nanocomposite membranes are fabricated from two or more polymers and their structure including a thin polymer barrier layer formed on one or more porous support layers [164].

Fig. 3 (a) illustrates the major polymers that have been used in TFN fabrication process. The data indicates that the main polymer used in TFN synthesis is the PA (94.9%). This polymer accounts for more than 94% of all TFN membranes and the remainder of membranes spread across a further 138 different articles. The trend in TFN research using PA has been generally increasing since 2011 which is indicated in Fig. 3 (b).

The fabrication method is an important aspect of membrane design that will dictate the economics of TFNs. An ultrathin and dense selective film of nanoparticles can be coated on the porous sub-layer with different procedures. Interfacial polymerization has been developed as a powerful method to synthesize widespread functional polymers [165]. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the interfacial polymerization process has been broadly used by researchers, displaying growth over the period reviewed, see Fig. 4 (b).

The dispersed location of nanoparticles in TFN membranes to enhance performance havealsoexamined in this study. Fig. 5 represents the number of publications that investigated the addition of nanomaterials into organic or aqueous solutions for TFN fabrication process. The trend for dispersing particles in aqueous solution indicates a significant growth comparing with the organic solution from 2011 to present. However, excellent dispersion of nanoparticles in an organic solvent/aqueous solution is difficult to achieve TFN membrane fabrication process [166].

In general, TFN membranes are prepared by the reaction of a diamine in an aqueous-phase solution with an acid chloride in an organic-phase solution [167]. Fig. 6 shows the distribution of aqueous solutions that are utilized in the TFNs fabrication process. M-phenylenediamine-based (MPD) and piperazine-based (PIP) membranes were the most studied as aqueous solutions by researchers in recent years. For obtaining higher rejection performance in PIP membranes, it was reported that the presence of an acid acceptor in an aqueous solution is necessary. However, this was not reported for the MPD membranes [168]. Moreover, the reaction rate of a PIP solution into the trimesoyl chloride solution is low. In order to increase the activity layer of polyamide, there is a need for acyl halide along with an acid acceptor in higher concentration value. But in the case of MPD based membranes, the high portion of tertiary amine content acts as a built-in acid acceptor [169]. The trend of Fig. 6 (b) shows an increasing growth of these two common reactive monomers from 2015 to present. In this study, among the identified articles [1,7–15,17,18,20,41–156] in the period of interest, there were several different solutes. A list of solutes that used to test the membrane performance was ranked based on the number of membranes fabricated, see Table 2 . In total, there were 331 different membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison of thin-film nanocomposite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Although, polymeric membranes have been widely applied for the separation process, their performance is limited by the tradeoff relationship between permeability and selectivity. This tradeoff in gas separation applications can be presented by Robeson’s upper bound [170].
However, such upper bound concept may not be restricted to gas separation membranes and can be extended to water separation as well and we explore this within this study.

Geise et al. studied the general tradeoff relations between water permeability and water/salt selectivity and observed an upper bound behavior [171]. As water permeability (L m−2h−1) is given in the literature with different pressure, herein, water permeance (L m−2h−1 bar−1) was calculated based on the test operating pressure and water flux reported in the literature.

Fig. 7 shows the correlation between NaCl separation factor (1/
S a
)
and water permeance based on a set of 495 data points for TFN PA membranes collected from the literature. Given that no data was found in the upper right-hand corner in the figure, we believe that this may be possible due to an upper bound. Similarly, the comparison of TFN PA membranes using 87 different types of fillers separated based on different categories (Table 3 ) are presented in Fig. 7. These different fillers may have a significant effect on the performance of the membranes. It is clear from Fig. 7 that an upper bound line can be established for all the different categories of fillers using 690 data sets attained with the 87 different fillers that we categorize into 12 groups.
The water permeance-separation factor plots for TFN PA membranes Na2SO4, MgSO4 and MgCl2 with and without nanoparticles (based on the literature survey of TFN membranes) are demonstrated in Fig. 8 .

There is a general trend of decreased MgCl2, MgSO4 separation factor and increased Na2SO4 for more permeable membranes. The upper bound showed in Fig. 8. can be used as a reference for membrane separation properties in future projects.

It is necessary to assess the correlation between each pair of input variables within ML models as high correlation coefficients between input variables of ANN models may cause overfitting [161]. Pearson's correlation coefficients between each pair of input variables of the ANN model for permeate flux and foulant rejection are shown in Table 4 and Table 5 . The very low values of correlation coefficients (less than 0.6) result in no correlation between the inputs of the proposed ANN models. Therefore, all the nine input variables of the ANN models including support, particles concentration, concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane), operation pressure, contact angle, thin layer thickness, location of the NPs, post treatment temperature and post treatment duration were selected for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection.

To obtain the minimum error (the difference between the targets and outputs) of ANN models for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection, the number neurons in the hidden layer and initial weights of the ANN models were optimized. The number of neurons in the hidden layer was varied between 5 and 50, in which 50 ANN models with different initial weights were validated and compared to find the best models for each output. Overall, 2250 ANN models were evaluated and compared for estimation of permeate flux and 2250 ANN models for foulant rejection.

Table 6 shows the MSE and R2 of training, validation and test datasets of the ANN models on the estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection based on the different number of neurons in the hidden layer. The MSE and R2 values for permeate flux are between 0.8272 to 62.9772 and 0.8759 to 0.9984 for training, 3.9711 to 818.0208 and 0.2103 to 0.9887 for validation and 4.5231 to 74.7434 and 0.8364 to 0.9871 for test datasets, respectively. In addition, the MSE and R2 values for foulant rejection ranges from 2.1279 to 22.6478 and 0.7408 to 0.9720 for training, 3.4898 to 37.4896 and 0.2744 to 0.9751 for validation, and for 3.7224 to 34.9129 and 0.8818 to 0.9661 for test datasets, respectively. As can be seen from Table 6, the differences between the R2 of training, validation and test datasets are less than 0.05, which indicates that there is no overfitting of models. Typically, an ANN model is considered to be not over-fitted if R values of training and validation dataset are similar to each other [161]. The best 20 ANN models for permeate flux and foulant rejection were then selected for further analysis. Fig. 9 depicts the regression plots of the best 20 ANN models for permeate flux and foulant rejection on training, validation and test datasets. The results show the R2 value of 0.9958 for estimation of permeate flux and R2 value of 0.9412 for estimation of foulant rejection. Furthermore, the slope and offset of regression plots for permeate flux are 0.9925 and 0.3080 and for foulant rejection are 0.9178 and 7.6226 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the artificial neural network models using unseen data
ce:section.ce:para: While the optimized ANN models developed in the previous section estimate the permeate flux and foulant rejection accurately on the training, validation and test datasets, it’s necessary to keep the high estimation performance on a new dataset which is not included in the training, validation and test stages [34]. Accordingly, the best 20 trained ANN models were tested on unseen data, which was derived from experimentations in the literature, for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection. The average of the output of the 20 best ANN models was used to display the regression plots and the comparison of estimated and experimental value as shown in Fig. 10 and Fig. 11 .

Fig. 10 (a) and Fig. 10 (b) depict the regression plots of the unseen data for permeate flux and foulant rejection, respectively. The results show R2 value of 0.9938 with the slope and offset of 0.9799 and 0.9555 for permeate flux, and R2 value of 0.9811 with the slope and offset of 0.9695 and 2.7343 for foulant rejection. Fig. 11 (a) and Fig. 11 (b) show the comparison of the estimated and experimental values on the unseen data for permeate flux and foulant rejection respectively. The results demonstrate that the proposed ANN models can estimate permeate flux and foulant rejection values with root mean squared error of (RMSE) of 2.20 and 0.932 respectively.

Sensitivity analysis was then employed to identify the order of importance of input variables of the ANN models for estimation of permeate flux and foulant rejection. The positive value of
R
I i
shows a positive correlation between the ith input variable and the corresponding output. However, the negative value of
I i
demonstrates a negative correlation between the ith input variable and the corresponding output. The higher absolute value of the
R
I i
, the more sensitive the ith input variable. Fig. 12 (a) depicts the order of importance of the nine input variables for estimation of permeate flux. The results show that the order of importance of input variables for permeate flux is: 1) post treatment temperature, 2) contact angle, 3) location of the NPs, 4) support, 5) operation pressure, 6) post treatment duration, 7) thin layer thickness, 8) particles concentration and 9) concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane).

Fig. 12 (b) shows the order of importance of the nine input variables for estimation of foulant rejection. It can be seen that the order of importance of input variables for foulant rejection is: 1) contact angle, 2) post treatment temperature, 3) operation pressure, 4) thin layer thickness, 5) concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane), 6) post treatment duration, 7) support, 8) particles concentration and 9) location of the NPs

The post treatment temperature and contact angle were identified as the most important and effective input variable of the ANN models with
R
I i
of 1.1780 and −0.5589 for permeate flux and
R
I i
of −0.0130 and −0.0230 for foulant rejection respectively.

The concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane) and particles concentration were found as the least important input variables of the ANN models with
R
I i
of 0.0096 and 0.0210 for permeate flux, concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane) and particles concentration as the least effective input variables with
R
I i
of 0.0024 and −0.0003 for foulant rejection.

In this study, we presented a ML approach to estimate permeate flux and foulant rejection for nanocomposite filtration membranes based on a range of input variables: support, particles concentration, concentration of organic phase (TMC in n-hexane), operation pressure, contact angle, thin layer thickness, location of the NPs, post treatment temperature and post treatment duration. The Pearson's correlation coefficients between each pair of input variables were evaluated to find and remove the correlated inputs of the ANN models. The number of the neurons in the hidden layer and the initial weights of the ANN models were then optimized to maximize R2 and minimize MSE. Totally 2250 ANN models for each of permeate flux and foulant rejection were evaluated and the 20 best ANN models were selected for further analysis. The results showed that ANN models are efficient methods to estimate permeate flux and foulant rejection with high level of accuracy and generalizability on training, validation, test and unseen datasets. Furthermore, sensitivity analysis was employed to determine the order of importance of the input variables of the ANN models. Post treatment temperature and contact angle were found the most important input variables. The proposed method can be used for formulating permeate flux and foulant rejection and considering the effects of each experimental condition on nanocomposite filtration membranes without doing real experiments which is time-consuming and expensive.
